in_groups: Observer at the edge. She goes to the park where older kids hang out on Friday nights and stands near enough to feel cool, far enough to flee. In class she tracks who's watching — Mr. Procházka asking her to help with pronunciation makes her skin crawl because attention is a spotlight and spotlights find the cracks. She learned this at nursery: be small, be near the fish tank, be present without being seen. The canteen table near the bins. The back row near the radiator. These are her positions. She fills silences with chip-eating so nobody feels obligated to talk, and she doesn't know if that's kindness or camouflage.
one_on_one: Depends entirely on who. With Tereza: loose, code-switching, honest in the way that feels like breathing — 'that's fucked' when things are fucked, no performance. With Karolína: clean and focused, talks splits and race strategy, the relationship exists in motion because stillness is where feelings live. With Babička: silent, eating, present without pressure — she learned this from Děda, how to be with someone without needing anything. With Mum: defended, armed, capable of cruelty she hates herself for — 'you chose this life, I didn't.' With Matěj: guarded now, checking her own voice, aware of the territory his feelings have claimed. She protects herself by controlling proximity. If she can't control proximity, she controls visibility. If she can't control visibility, she leaves.
conflict_style: Deflects with cruelty or silence, never with vulnerability. She learned from the bathroom door — Mum's crying behind it, Anna's hand frozen before the knock. Conflict that's loud she can handle (she decided crying should be loud or it doesn't count). Conflict that's quiet destroys her. So she makes it loud, or she makes it mean, or she makes it disappear by leaving the room. The 'good for you' to Tereza about Ondřej — deflection that hurt more than honesty. The 'I didn't ask you to leave England' to Klára — the blade drawn because the alternative was feeling the wound. She fights with weapons she hates using. She doesn't know another way to get space from someone who won't give it willingly.
affection_style: Presence without declaration. She wears the friendship bracelet from Elara Chung until it's grey and fraying — she's never said what it means. She sits in Babička's kitchen without speaking and lets the warmth fill her. She runs alongside Karolína for hours and calls it training. The hug after the championship time trial was the most sincere physical contact she's initiated because it happened before she could stop herself, in a moment of triumph, not crisis. She doesn't say 'I love you' in any language. She shows up. She stays. She sits across from you for three hours without needing anything back — this is the love she learned from a dead man and a chess set.
stress_response: Evacuation. Physical distance is her only reliable tool. When Mum's anxiety fills the room, Anna leaves. When Pavel's coat appears on the hook, she retreats to Babička's kitchen or the attic or the field. When the radiator knocked, she pulled the pillow over her head. Running isn't exercise — it's the off switch, the only way to outrun the thing that's chasing her. Under pressure she gets short ('fine,' 'yeah,' 'okay') and her body moves before her mind decides — the bite on Krish Patel, the 'Dad' slip, the 'okay' to Lukáš Fiala that she replayed every night for a month. She doesn't confront stress. She outlasts it or outruns it, and when she can't do either, she becomes the stone — hard, contained, the thing that doesn't cry at funerals.
decision_making: Avoidant with eruptions of impulse she can't predict or control. She avoids decisions she can't solve (Lukáš, Matěj's feelings, the Christmas visit to Dad — 'I'm busy' was easier than going back to that flat). She dodges FaceTime calls and feels sick about it for days and still doesn't call back. But then her body decides without her — teeth in Krish Patel's arm before she knew they'd moved, the word 'Dad' leaving her mouth at dinner before she could catch it, the 'okay' that shut Lukáš down before she could think. She doesn't choose these moments. They happen to her. The gap between what she decides and what her body does terrifies her in a way she's never articulated — that the line between her and not-her isn't as clear as she thought.
